rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, NATIONAL ASSOCIATION VS. MIMI WONG et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 29, 2025, line 1, PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, NATIONAL ASSOCIATION'S HEARING ON CLAIM OF EXEMPTION The court is inclined to grant the claim of exemption, but a hearing is required. The parties are directed to appear. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. If the court adopts the tentative, the court will execute a judicial council form of order. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CV)

rulings.Case Title: HOWARD I. MAIBACH VS. THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, A
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Application For Leave To File Special Motion To Strike Pursuant To Ccp Section 425.16
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 29, 2025, line 6, RESPONDENT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA Amended Notice Of Application For Leave To File Special Motion To Strike Pursuant To Ccp Section 425.16 (tentative ruling part 1 OF 2) Respondent's Application For Leave To File Special Motion To Strike is denied. On November 9, 2023, Petitioner Howard Maibach filed this petition for a writ of mandate against the Regents of the University of California to enforce the California Public Records Act's requirement to produce documents. Petitioner claims that UCSF published a report that Petitioner's research raised ethical concerns and perpetuated racism. Petitioner wants to force UCSF to disclose documents withheld by the university related to the investigation underlying the report. The petition was served on November 13, 2023. CCP Section 425.16(f) provides that: "The special motion may be filed within 60 days of the service of the complaint or, in the court's discretion, at any later time upon terms it deems proper. The motion shall be scheduled by the clerk of the court for a hearing not more than 30 days after the service of the motion unless the docket conditions of the court require a later hearing." The deadline here was January 12, 2024 absent the exercise of this court's discretion. In considering whether to grant leave for this significant extension, the court considers UCSF's reasons for delay and the prejudice to Maibach. The prejudice is significant: Maibach's CPRA action should be a summary proceeding resulting in a quick judicial determination. Allowing a special motion to strike could delay the action significantly in light of the potential appeal that would follow. Moreover, the parties have already invested significant resources in discovery. One benefit of the anti-SLAPP motion is that it typically precedes discovery and sometimes obviates the need for it; that benefit is lost where a party delays bringing the motion. UCSF's justification is not strong. UCSF posits that the need to file an anti-SLAPP motion only became apparent recently in discovery when Maibach contended the UCSF report was not legitimate academic research. That contention appeared on the face of the petition (e.g. Petn. para. 11), and UCSF's answer acknowledged the availability of the anti-SLAPP motion (Ans. at 1:6-9). That UCSF would now make a different strategic choice about filing an anti-SLAPP motion does not in itself justify the late filing. (END OF TENTATIVE RULING PART1 SEE PART 2) = (301/CV)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Default Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 29, 2025, line 2, DEFENDANT SUBSTACK INC'S Motion To Vacate Default Judgment Plaintiff Brian Collins, acting in pro per, failed to file his opposition papers with this court. Defendant Substack has provided a copy with its reply brief. The court exercises its discretion to consider the arguments in opposition made by Collins and grants Substack's motion to vacate the default judgment. Substack challenges the personal and subject-matter jurisdiction of the New Hampshire court that rendered a sister-state default judgment against it. A default judgment may be vacated where the court lacked personal jurisdiction over the defendant, including where the defendant was not served with process. "When a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction on the ground of improper service of process, the burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service." (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413 [internal quotation marks, citation, and footnote omitted].) Similarly, "[w]hen a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction [on the basis of lack of contacts with the forum state], the plaintiff has the initial burden of 'demonstrating facts justifying the exercise of jurisdiction.' " (Strathvale Holdings v. E.B.H. (2005) 126 Cal.App.4th 1241, 1250 [quoting Vons Companies, Inc. v. Seabest Foods, Inc. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 434, 449.) Here, Substack challenges both service and the New Hampshire court's exercise of personal jurisdiction over it, placing the burden on Collins to provide evidence (beyond conclusory assertions) of service and jurisdiction. Collins has presented no competent evidence to carry that burden. The court therefore grants Substack's motion and vacates the judgment pursuant to CCP 1710.25 entered by this court on March 5, 2024. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Renewed Motion And Motion To Compel Amended Privilege Log And Production
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery/Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 29, 2025, line 2, PLAINTIFFS STEVEN FREDIANI, ROBERT RIGLEY, RICHARD TROCHE, MICHAEL DION'S Notice Of Renewed Motion And Motion To Compel Amended Privilege Log And Production The underlying case stems from defendant Pacific Gas & Electric Company's termination of plaintiffs. This motion concerns an amended privilege log, attached as Exh. D to the Farrow Declaration (submitted by PG&E in opposition). The amended log was prepared on October 21, 2024 after an earlier discovery motion. Plaintiffs now seek (1) an amended privilege log; and (2) compelled further production of documents where they contend PG&E has waived its privilege. rulings.Case Title: CAMAL EL SHERIFI ET AL VS. MERCY HOUSING CALIFORNIA VI ASSET MANAGEMENT, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs' Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One From Defendant Mercy Housing Management Group, Inc.; Request For Monetary Sanctions (Amended)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, January 29, 2025, line 1, PLAINTIFFS CAMAL EL SHERIFI, DAVID BAKER, LEA CURRY, MOHAMED SADI, MOHAMED ABID, MOUSLIH NEZHA, CELSO BORGES' Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs' Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One From Defendant Mercy Housing Management Group, Inc.; Request For Monetary Sanctions (Amended) Plaintiff's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Plaintiffs' Requests for Production of Documents, Set One from Defendant Mercy Housing Management Group, Inc. is continued to February 28, 2025. Plaintiff filed this motion on October 24, 2024, and then re-calendared it without changing the substance of the motion. Defendant produced additional documents on October 29 and 30 and on November 4. (Supp. Darsky Decl. 6-7.) Plaintiff admits that defendant supplemented its responses to Nos. 5, 7, 12, 21, 25, 32, 45, 47, and 48 and refused to supplement its responses to Nos. 23, 8, 43, 15, and 16. (Supp. Darsky Decl. 7.) The court orders the parties to continue to meet and confer regarding whether the supplemented responses moot at least a portion of this motion and whether a privilege log should be produced. Plaintiff may file a 5-page supplemental brief and an updated separate statement by February 10. Defendant may file a 5-page supplemental brief and opposing separate statement by February 20. The court notes that the sanctions issue is still live. (See CRC 3.1348(a) [court may award sanctions where discovery is provided after a motion is filed].) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number.
